title: Finance and Economics Discussion Series (FEDS) - 2025
summary: This is an archive listing page for the Federal Reserve's 2025 Finance and Economics Discussion Series (FEDS), containing approximately 10+ research papers. Only one paper (FEDS 2025-106) is directly relevant to market structure, covering how 'funds of funds' mechanically rebalanced portfolios during the COVID-19 crisis and transmitted shocks across markets. All other papers cover unrelated topics (labor economics, data center investment, machine learning, AI patents, payment systems).
key_points: FEDS 2025-106 examines how 'funds of funds' (mutual funds investing in other mutual funds) mechanically rebalanced during the COVID-19 crisis, accounting for a third of bond fund outflows in March 2020
These funds sold 14% of total bond fund holdings but concentrated sales in government bond funds, liquidating 34% of those holdings
The paper finds that mechanical portfolio rebalancing can transmit shocks across markets and generate destabilizing effects
The source is a listing/archive page, not a single archiveable document
No individual paper is sufficiently developed in this listing to serve as a standalone source on market structure or macro catalysts
why_it_matters: This listing page provides access to Federal Reserve research but is not itself an archiveable research document. The single market-structure-relevant paper (FEDS 2025-106) requires retrieval as a standalone document to be useful for RAG purposes.
market_structure_context: FEDS 2025-106 documents how fund-of-funds rebalancing during March 2020 transmitted shocks across markets by mechanically selling bond funds—particularly government bonds—after equity losses, representing a form of market plumbing vulnerability. However, this finding must be extracted from the individual paper, not this listing page.
